---
title: "Diffeomorphic registration: velocity fields that do not tear"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
This is the top of the [transform ladder](foundations.qmd#sec-ladder) — the
*deformable* rung, where the map is a dense per-voxel field rather than a small
matrix. A global affine can centre a subject's brain in MNI space, but it cannot
match one person's sulci to another's: that needs a transform with a free vector
at every voxel. The danger is that such a map can **tear or fold** — two voxels
landing on the same place, the warp turning anatomy inside out. The single idea
that buys safety: don't parametrise the *displacement* and hope it stays
invertible; parametrise a **velocity field** and *flow along it*. A flow can
stretch arbitrarily but never crosses itself, so its endpoint map is a
diffeomorphism **by construction**.

This page owns the whole deformable path end-to-end:

- **displacement vs absolute coordinates**, and why composing two warps is *not*
  adding their fields (`compose_displacement`, `jacobian_det_displacement`);
- **stationary velocity fields**, the exponential `exp(v)` by
  scaling-and-squaring (`integrate_velocity_field`), and the two ways to carry the
  state (`representation='algebra'` vs `'group'`);
- the **log-domain algebra** — composing velocities (`compose_velocity`, the BCH
  correction), inverting a warp (`invert_displacement`), and the SVF logarithm
  (`field_log`), with the crucial caveat that `exp` is not onto;
- the **regularisers** that keep the map physical — explicit penalties
  (`gradient_smoothness`, `bending_energy`, `jacobian_folding_penalty`) and the
  implicit Gaussian smoothing the recipes use;
- the **force ADT** and the dense-field solvers (`DemonsForce`, `LNCCForce`,
  `MIForce`, `MetricForce`, `SumForce`) and the recipes that drive them
  (`diffeomorphic_demons_register`, `greedy_syn_register`).

If you read the [optimization page](optimization.qmd#sec-objective) for the
matrix recipes, this is its dense-field sibling: same coarse-to-fine spine,
different state.
:::

## A motivating question {#sec-motivation}

Hold one concrete task for the whole page. You have two subjects'
**T1-weighted scans**, already roughly aligned by a rigid-plus-affine stage (the
[matrix rungs](transforms.qmd#sec-decomposition)), and you want to bring one
subject's cortex into precise correspondence with the other's — gyrus on gyrus,
sulcus in sulcus — so you can compare cortical thickness or pool a statistical map
across a cohort in a common template. The affine got the brains the same size and
position; it cannot do the rest, because **individual folding patterns differ**.
Subject A has a bifurcating superior frontal sulcus where subject B has a single
deep one. No rotation, scaling, or shear of the whole head moves A's sulcus onto
B's without dragging the rest of the cortex out of register. We need a transform
with a *local* degree of freedom — a displacement vector at every voxel.

That richness is also the hazard. A free field of $N$ vectors over $N$ voxels can
do anything, including the one thing a map between anatomies must never do:
**fold**. If the deformation pushes two neighbouring voxels past each other, the
map is no longer injective — two bits of subject A's brain land on the same bit of
B's, the local volume goes to zero and then negative, and "cortical thickness at
this point" stops meaning anything. We want the expressive power of a dense field
*and* the guarantee that the map is a **diffeomorphism**: smooth, invertible, with
a smooth inverse. That is the whole subject of this page. We will build it the way
the unit always does — start with the obvious thing, watch it break, add one idea.

## Displacement, absolute coordinates, and composition {#sec-displacement}

The obvious representation of a deformable map is a **displacement field**
$u(x)$: at each voxel $x$, the vector you add to get where it goes,
$$
\varphi(x) = x + u(x).
$$ {#eq-disp}
This is the **relative** convention, and it is the one every public field in this
module uses: a channels-last array of shape `(*spatial, ndim)`, one displacement
vector per voxel, in **index space** (voxel $i$ sits at coordinate $i$;
`indexing='ij'`, the convention from the [geometry
unit](../geometry/foundations.qmd)). The displacement of the identity map is the
zero field.

To *apply* a warp we must sample the moving image at the deformed positions — the
[pull-back](foundations.qmd#sec-pullback). But a sampler reads from **absolute**
coordinates, not relative ones: `spatial_transform(image, deformation)` (the
[backward-warp engine](resampling.qmd#sec-pullback-sampling)) treats
`deformation` as the coordinate to sample at, so you must hand it the *absolute*
field
$$
\text{deformation}(x) = x + u(x) = \texttt{identity\_grid}(\text{shape}) + u,
$$ {#eq-abs}
where `identity_grid(shape)` (**DIFF-JAX**, from `geometry.grid`) is exactly the
field `grid[i] == i`. This split — relative $u$ for storage and algebra, absolute
`identity_grid + u` at the sampler boundary — is a constant background motif; every
routine below pays it. The displacement is what you carry, regularise, and
exponentiate; the absolute grid is what the sampler consumes.

### Composing two warps is not adding their fields {#sec-compose}

Here is the first place the naive instinct fails. You have two displacement
fields — warp by `inner` first, then by `outer` — and you want the single field
that does both. The tempting answer is `inner + outer`. It is wrong, and the
reason is the heart of why deformable registration has its own algebra.

Composition means *function* composition: the combined map is
$(\mathrm{id} + \text{outer}) \circ (\mathrm{id} + \text{inner})$. Track a point.
First `inner` sends $x \mapsto x + \text{inner}(x)$. Then `outer` acts **at that
new location**, not at $x$: it adds $\text{outer}(x + \text{inner}(x))$. So
$$
\varphi(x) = x + \text{inner}(x) + \text{outer}\bigl(x + \text{inner}(x)\bigr),
$$ {#eq-compose}
and the composed *displacement* is
$\text{inner} + \text{outer}\circ(\mathrm{id} + \text{inner})$ — the second field
**sampled through the first**, not added to it. That sampling is exactly what
`compose_displacement(outer, inner)` (**DIFF-JAX**, `geometry.deformation`)
computes:

```python
grid = identity_grid(spatial_shape) + inner
warped_outer = spatial_transform(outer, grid, mode='nearest')
return inner + warped_outer
```

The elementwise sum `inner + outer` only equals this when `outer` is constant over
the support of `inner` — i.e. essentially never for two non-trivial warps.
@fig-diffeomorphic-compose makes the gap concrete: two shears that do not commute
(`inner` shears axis 0 by axis 1, `outer` shears axis 1 by axis 0). The true
composition curves; the elementwise sum is a flat parallelogram; the overlay shows
several voxels of disagreement. The `mode='nearest'` default (edge-replicate) is
the flow-field convention — a displacement field has no meaningful "outside", so
sampling past the edge clamps rather than zero-fills, matching
`integrate_velocity_field` and the rest of the SVF stack.

```{python}
#| label: fig-diffeomorphic-compose
#| fig-cap: "Composing warps is function composition, not addition. Two non-commuting shears, composed via `compose_displacement` (left, blue) versus added elementwise (centre, red); the overlay (right) shows a multi-voxel gap. The elementwise sum is wrong because the outer warp acts at the *deformed* location, so it must be sampled through the inner warp."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

H = W = 64
ii, jj = np.meshgrid(np.arange(H), np.arange(W), indexing="ij")
grid = np.stack([ii, jj], axis=-1).astype(np.float64)

def sample(field, coords):
    Hh, Ww = field.shape[:2]
    x = np.clip(coords[..., 0], 0, Hh - 1)
    y = np.clip(coords[..., 1], 0, Ww - 1)
    x0 = np.floor(x).astype(int); x1 = np.minimum(x0 + 1, Hh - 1)
    y0 = np.floor(y).astype(int); y1 = np.minimum(y0 + 1, Ww - 1)
    wx = (x - x0)[..., None]; wy = (y - y0)[..., None]
    return ((1 - wx) * (1 - wy) * field[x0, y0] + (1 - wx) * wy * field[x0, y1]
            + wx * (1 - wy) * field[x1, y0] + wx * wy * field[x1, y1])

def compose_displacement(outer, inner):  # (id+outer) o (id+inner)
    return inner + sample(outer, grid + inner)

c = np.array([(H - 1) / 2.0, (W - 1) / 2.0])
rel = grid - c
k = 0.45
inner = rel @ np.array([[0.0, k], [0.0, 0.0]]).T   # shear x0 by x1
outer = rel @ np.array([[0.0, 0.0], [k, 0.0]]).T   # shear x1 by x0

composed = compose_displacement(outer, inner)
naive_sum = outer + inner
rms = float(np.sqrt(np.mean(np.sum((composed - naive_sum) ** 2, axis=-1))))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11.0, 3.7))
def plot_warp(ax, disp, title, color):
    d = grid + disp
    for r in range(0, H, 4):
        ax.plot(d[r, :, 1], d[r, :, 0], color=color, lw=0.6)
    for cc in range(0, W, 4):
        ax.plot(d[:, cc, 1], d[:, cc, 0], color=color, lw=0.6)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlim(-18, W + 18); ax.set_ylim(H + 18, -18)
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])

plot_warp(axes[0], composed, "compose_displacement(outer, inner)\n(id+outer) ∘ (id+inner)", "#1f77b4")
plot_warp(axes[1], naive_sum, "outer + inner\n(elementwise sum — wrong)", "#d62728")
plot_warp(axes[2], composed, "overlay", "#1f77b4")
plot_warp(axes[2], naive_sum, f"overlay: RMS gap = {rms:.1f} vox", "#d62728")
fig.suptitle("Composing warps is not adding displacements", fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.93])
plt.show()
```

### Detecting a fold: the Jacobian determinant {#sec-jacdet}

When does a displacement field stop being a valid map? When it folds — when the
local volume element collapses. The map's local linearisation is its **Jacobian**
$$
J = \nabla\varphi = I + \nabla u,
$$ {#eq-jac}
the matrix of partial derivatives of the deformed coordinate. The map is locally
orientation-preserving (no fold) exactly when $\det J > 0$; $\det J = 1$ is
locally volume-preserving; $\det J \to 0$ is a local collapse and $\det J < 0$ is a
fold, where anatomy turns inside out. This is *the* diffeomorphism QA: a warp is a
diffeomorphism on the grid iff $\det J > 0$ at **every** voxel.

`jacobian_displacement(u)` (**DIFF-JAX**, `geometry.grid`) builds $I + \nabla u$ by
central differences along each spatial axis, returning the full
`(*spatial, d, d)` Jacobian; `boundary_mode` (`'nearest'` default, the voxelmorph
QA convention) and per-axis `spacing` control the difference stencil.
`jacobian_det_displacement(u)` (**DIFF-JAX**) returns just the per-voxel
determinant, and is the one you actually call — for $d \le 3$ it uses the
closed-form $2\times2$ / Sarrus $3\times3$ determinant (no LU factorisation noise,
no $O(d^3)$ cost), falling back to `jnp.linalg.det` only for $d > 3$. Every recipe
returns this field as `jacobian_det`; the standard acceptance test is *"no voxels
with $\det J \le 0$."* @fig-diffeomorphic-jacdet shows a gentle pinch (positive
everywhere — a diffeomorphism) beside a strong one that folds (a ring of
$\det J \le 0$ voxels, outlined).

```{python}
#| label: fig-diffeomorphic-jacdet
#| fig-cap: "The Jacobian determinant det(I + ∇u) is the folding detector. A gentle pinch (left) keeps det J > 0 everywhere — a diffeomorphism. A strong pinch (right) drives det J ≤ 0 in a ring (black contour): the map folds and is no longer invertible there. Every recipe returns this field as `jacobian_det`; the acceptance test is 'no voxels with det J ≤ 0'."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.colors import TwoSlopeNorm

H = W = 80
ii, jj = np.meshgrid(np.arange(H), np.arange(W), indexing="ij")
grid = np.stack([ii, jj], axis=-1).astype(np.float64)
c = np.array([(H - 1) / 2.0, (W - 1) / 2.0])
g = np.exp(-np.sum((grid - c) ** 2, axis=-1) / (2.0 * 12.0 ** 2))
direction = np.zeros_like(grid); direction[..., 0] = grid[..., 0] - c[0]

def displacement(amp):
    return -amp * direction * g[..., None]

def central_diff(field, axis):
    nxt = np.roll(field, -1, axis=axis); prv = np.roll(field, +1, axis=axis)
    sl_last = [slice(None)] * field.ndim; sl_last[axis] = -1
    sl_first = [slice(None)] * field.ndim; sl_first[axis] = 0
    nxt[tuple(sl_last)] = field[tuple(sl_last)]
    prv[tuple(sl_first)] = field[tuple(sl_first)]
    return (nxt - prv) / 2.0

def jac_det(u):  # det(I + grad u), 2x2 closed form
    d0 = central_diff(u, 0); d1 = central_diff(u, 1)
    return (1.0 + d0[..., 0]) * (1.0 + d1[..., 1]) - d1[..., 0] * d0[..., 1]

det_smooth = jac_det(displacement(0.30))
det_fold = jac_det(displacement(1.25))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.4, 3.8))
norm = TwoSlopeNorm(vmin=min(det_fold.min(), -0.5), vcenter=0.0,
                    vmax=max(det_smooth.max(), det_fold.max(), 2.0))
for ax, det, title in (
    (axes[0], det_smooth, f"smooth warp\nmin det J = {det_smooth.min():.2f} > 0  (diffeomorphism)"),
    (axes[1], det_fold, f"folding warp\nmin det J = {det_fold.min():.2f} ≤ 0  (tears)"),
):
    im = ax.imshow(det, cmap="RdBu", norm=norm)
    ax.contour(det, levels=[0.0], colors="k", linewidths=1.4)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10); ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
cbar = fig.colorbar(im, ax=axes, shrink=0.85, label="det J = det(I + ∇u)")
cbar.ax.axhline(0.0, color="k", lw=1.2)
fig.suptitle("Jacobian determinant detects folding (det J ≤ 0 ⇒ the map tears)", fontsize=11)
plt.show()
```

A field stored as a raw displacement gives us no *guarantee* against this; we can
only check after the fact. The next section removes the check by changing what we
parametrise.

## Stationary velocity fields and the exponential {#sec-svf}

Here is the idea that makes the guarantee structural rather than hoped-for.
Instead of writing down the displacement directly, describe the **velocity** of a
flow. Picture every voxel as a particle in a steady fluid. A **stationary velocity
field** $v(x)$ assigns each spatial location a fixed velocity vector — fixed in
*space*, so a particle's velocity depends only on where it currently is, not on
the clock. Release the particles and let them drift for unit time. Where each one
ends up defines the deformation
$$
\varphi = \exp(v),
$$ {#eq-exp}
the **flow** (or Lie exponential) of $v$. This is the same object as the matrix
exponential from the [transforms page](transforms.qmd#sec-lie-exp), lifted from a
$d\times d$ generator to a dense field of generators — one tangent vector per
voxel instead of one for the whole image.

Why does flowing guarantee a diffeomorphism? Because a steady flow's trajectories
**cannot cross**. If two particles were ever at the same place at the same time
they would have the same velocity and the same future and past — so they were
always the same particle. Distinct start points stay distinct, the endpoint map is
a bijection, and because $v$ is smooth the flow and its inverse are smooth. The map
is a diffeomorphism *by construction*: not "we checked $\det J$ and it happened to
be positive," but "no field of finite velocity, flowed for finite time, can fold."
That is the entire payoff. The price is that we now have to *integrate* the flow.

### Computing the flow: scaling and squaring {#sec-ss}

Integrating an ODE sounds expensive. The trick that makes it cheap — and
differentiable, and GPU-clean — is **scaling and squaring**, the same algorithm
used for the matrix exponential. It rests on one identity of flows: flowing for
time 1 equals flowing for time $1/2$ and then *doing it again*,
$$
\exp(v) = \exp(v/2) \circ \exp(v/2).
$$ {#eq-halving}
Run this backwards. Scale $v$ down by $2^n$ until the sub-step is tiny — under
about half a voxel. Over so small a step the flow is indistinguishable from a
single Euler step, so $\exp(v/2^n) \approx \mathrm{id} + v/2^n$ is *already* an
(approximately) diffeomorphic displacement, with no integration needed. Then
recover the full flow by **squaring** — composing the field with itself — $n$
times, each composition doubling the integration time:
$$
\phi_0 = \frac{v}{2^n}, \qquad
\phi_{i+1} = \phi_i + \phi_i \circ (\mathrm{id} + \phi_i),
$$ {#eq-ss}
where the composition is precisely the `compose_displacement` operation of
@eq-compose (a field sampled through itself). After $n$ doublings, $\phi_n$ is the
displacement of $\exp(v)$. Each squaring is one resample; the cost is $n$ warps,
not an ODE solve.

This is `integrate_velocity_field(velocity, n_steps=7, mode='nearest')`
(**DIFF-JAX**, `geometry.grid`):

```python
phi0 = velocity / float(2**n_steps)
def _double(phi, _):
    return phi + spatial_transform(phi, id_grid + phi, mode=mode), None
phi, _ = jax.lax.scan(_double, phi0, xs=None, length=n_steps)
```

The doubling loop is a `lax.scan` over a **static** `n_steps`, so it compiles once
and stays differentiable — the diffeomorphic recipes nest this inside their
iteration loop, where an unrolled Python loop would multiply the compiled graph.
Two knobs earn comment:

- **`n_steps`** is the number of doublings (default 7, i.e. a $128\times$
  down-scale). More steps is more accurate but more compute (per-step accuracy
  roughly halves each doubling). It must be large enough that the *first* sub-step
  $\max\|v\|/2^n$ lands inside the $\sim$0.5-voxel regime where $\mathrm{id} + v/2^n$
  is itself invertible — below that the integrated flow can fold, defeating the
  whole construction. The rule of thumb $n \ge \lceil\log_2(2\max\|v\|)\rceil$ is
  what `n_steps='auto'` computes from the velocity. But `'auto'` reads
  $\max\|v\|$ as a concrete value to size the static scan, so it is **eager-only**:
  it cannot run under `jax.jit` (the scan length must be static, and a traced
  reduction cannot provide it), and the recipes always pass an explicit `int`. A
  warp larger than the grid can resolve folds at *any* `n_steps` — that is a
  resolution limit, not an integration one.
- **`mode='nearest'`** (edge-replicate) is the only boundary mode that yields a
  sensible flow at the image border; `'constant'` silently miscomputes the flow
  within $\sim n$ voxels of every edge. This default was deliberately flipped from
  the naive `'constant'` to match voxelmorph's `VecInt(method='ss')`.

@fig-diffeomorphic-ss integrates an analytic *rotational* velocity field (a rigid
rotation generator about the centre) at increasing `n_steps`. At `n_steps=0` the
bare first-order step $\mathrm{id}+v$ visibly shears the grid — the large
peripheral velocity over-shoots in a straight line; by `n_steps=6` the warp is a
clean rotation matching the closed-form flow (RMS error dropping from $\sim$1.6 to
$\sim$0.2 voxels).

```{python}
#| label: fig-diffeomorphic-ss
#| fig-cap: "Scaling-and-squaring converges to the true flow. An analytic rotational velocity field (a rotation generator about the centre) integrated at increasing n_steps. n_steps=0 is the bare first-order step id+v, which over-shoots the periphery in straight lines; each squaring doubles the integration time, and by n_steps=6 the warp matches the closed-form rotation (rightmost). RMS error to the exact flow shrinks monotonically."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

H = W = 64
ii, jj = np.meshgrid(np.arange(H), np.arange(W), indexing="ij")
grid = np.stack([ii, jj], axis=-1).astype(np.float64)
c = np.array([(H - 1) / 2.0, (W - 1) / 2.0])
omega = 0.35  # the flow's total turn (radians); generator A = omega * R90
rel = grid - c
v = omega * np.stack([-rel[..., 1], rel[..., 0]], axis=-1)

def sample(field, coords):
    Hh, Ww = field.shape[:2]
    x = np.clip(coords[..., 0], 0, Hh - 1); y = np.clip(coords[..., 1], 0, Ww - 1)
    x0 = np.floor(x).astype(int); x1 = np.minimum(x0 + 1, Hh - 1)
    y0 = np.floor(y).astype(int); y1 = np.minimum(y0 + 1, Ww - 1)
    wx = (x - x0)[..., None]; wy = (y - y0)[..., None]
    return ((1 - wx) * (1 - wy) * field[x0, y0] + (1 - wx) * wy * field[x0, y1]
            + wx * (1 - wy) * field[x1, y0] + wx * wy * field[x1, y1])

def scaling_and_squaring(vel, n):
    phi = vel / (2.0 ** n)
    for _ in range(n):
        phi = phi + sample(phi, grid + phi)
    return phi

ct, st = np.cos(omega), np.sin(omega)        # exp(A) is rotation by omega
true_disp = (rel @ np.array([[ct, -st], [st, ct]]).T) + c - grid

steps_list = [0, 1, 3, 6]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, len(steps_list) + 1, figsize=(3.0 * (len(steps_list) + 1), 3.2))
def plot_grid(ax, disp, title, color="#1f77b4"):
    d = grid + disp
    for r in range(0, H, 4):
        ax.plot(d[r, :, 1], d[r, :, 0], color=color, lw=0.6)
    for cc in range(0, W, 4):
        ax.plot(d[:, cc, 1], d[:, cc, 0], color=color, lw=0.6)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlim(-2, W + 1); ax.set_ylim(H + 1, -2)
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])

for ax, n in zip(axes[:-1], steps_list):
    disp = scaling_and_squaring(v, n)
    err = np.sqrt(np.mean(np.sum((disp - true_disp) ** 2, axis=-1)))
    plot_grid(ax, disp, f"n_steps = {n}\nRMS err = {err:.2f} vox")
plot_grid(axes[-1], true_disp, "true flow\n(closed-form)", color="#b30000")
fig.suptitle("Scaling-and-squaring: exp(v) converges to the true flow", fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.92])
plt.show()
```

### Two representations: algebra vs group {#sec-representation}

There is a choice in *what state the iteration carries*, exposed as
`SVFSpec.representation` and threaded through every SVF recipe. It is the
performance/exactness lever of the whole module, so it is worth understanding
before the recipes.

- **`'algebra'`** carries the stationary velocity $v$ and **re-exponentiates every
  iteration** (`integrate_velocity_field` inside the loop). The state lives in the
  Lie *algebra* (the tangent space of velocities); the update is additive there,
  $v \leftarrow v + u$, and the displacement $\exp(v)$ is recomputed from scratch
  each step. This is the **exact** SVF — byte-identical to the pre-v4 recipe, the
  parity oracle — but pays $\sim$7 gathers per iteration (the $n$-step
  scaling-and-squaring plus the warp).
- **`'group'`** (the default) carries the *displacement* $s$ directly — the state
  lives in the *group* of deformations — and uses the **greedy compositive
  update**: warp the moving image by $\mathrm{id}+s$ in one gather, then *compose*
  the regularised increment onto $s$ ($\varphi \leftarrow \varphi \circ
  (\mathrm{id}+\delta)$). No per-iteration `exp`; $\sim$2 gathers per iteration. The
  velocity, if you want it, is recovered **once** at finalisation via `field_log`.

The two are not the same fixed point — a greedy composition of many forces is not
generally $\exp$ of a single stationary velocity (next section) — so they agree on
*synthetic recovery* to tolerance, not field-for-field. The default is `'group'`
for speed; reach for `'algebra'` when you need the exact-SVF semantics or a parity
check.

## The log-domain algebra {#sec-bch}

To compose, invert, and average velocity fields we need to work in the **log
domain** — to do arithmetic on $v$ that corresponds to composition of the maps
$\exp(v)$. This is where the Lie structure stops being decoration and starts doing
work.

### Composing velocities: BCH {#sec-compose-vel}

Suppose you have two velocities $v$ and $u$ and want the single velocity $z$ whose
exponential is the composed map: $\exp(z) = \exp(v) \circ \exp(u)$. For *scalars*
(or commuting matrices) the answer is trivial: $e^v e^u = e^{v+u}$, so $z = v + u$.
The obvious thing is to add the velocities. And that is `compose_velocity(v, u,
order=1)` (**DIFF-JAX**, `geometry.deformation`): just $v + u$, the additive
log-domain update almost every diffeomorphic-Demons implementation uses.

It is **exact only when the fields commute**. In general two flows do not — flowing
along $v$ then $u$ lands somewhere different from $u$ then $v$ — and the discrepancy
is captured by the **Baker–Campbell–Hausdorff** series. The first correction is
$$
z = v + u + \tfrac{1}{2}[v, u] + \dots,
$$ {#eq-bch}
where $[v, u]$ is the **Lie bracket** of the two velocity fields. The bracket
measures exactly how much the two flows fail to commute. For velocity fields it is
the difference of directional derivatives,
$$
[v, u] = (v\cdot\nabla)\,u - (u\cdot\nabla)\,v,
$$ {#eq-bracket}
i.e. how much $u$ changes as you drift along $v$, minus how much $v$ changes as you
drift along $u$. `compose_velocity(v, u, order=2)` adds the
$\tfrac{1}{2}[v,u]$ term; the implementation forms each directional derivative as
the Jacobian of the field (recovered as $\nabla u = J - I$ from
`jacobian_displacement`) contracted against the other field. In the Demons recipe
this is the `bch_order` knob: `1` is the plain additive update (default), `2` the
BCH-corrected log-update — more faithful when the per-step force is large enough
that non-commutativity matters.

### Inverting a warp {#sec-inversion}

A diffeomorphism has an inverse; we frequently need it (the symmetric SyN
construction below builds $\varphi$ from a forward and an inverse half). For an SVF
the inverse is free in the log domain — $\exp(v)^{-1} = \exp(-v)$, flow backwards.
But when we carry the *displacement* (group mode) we need the inverse of a field
$\varphi = \mathrm{id} + s$ directly. The defining condition is that composing the
two gives the identity, $(\mathrm{id}+s)\circ(\mathrm{id}+s_{\text{inv}}) =
\mathrm{id}$. Expanding with @eq-compose and solving for $s_{\text{inv}}$ gives a
**fixed-point** equation:
$$
s_{\text{inv}} = -\,s \circ (\mathrm{id} + s_{\text{inv}}).
$$ {#eq-inv}
Read it as a recipe: a good inverse displacement is *minus the forward
displacement, sampled at where the inverse sends you*. Iterate it from
$s_{\text{inv}}=0$ and it converges — geometrically, at rate $\|\nabla s\|$. In the
**diffeomorphic regime** $\|\nabla s\| < 1$ (which the recipes' smoothing
guarantees) that is fast; the iteration plateaus only as $\|\nabla s\| \to 1$, a
stiff near-folding deformation.

`invert_displacement(s, ...)` (**DIFF-JAX**, `geometry.deformation`) solves this
via `numerics.fixed_point_solve`, so it is differentiable w.r.t. $s$ by the
**implicit-function theorem** (the gradient flows through the *solution*, not by
unrolling the iteration — O(1) memory in the iteration count). Two arguments are
about robustness:

- **`acceleration`** — `'picard'` (default) is the plain iteration above, fastest
  *and* most accurate on the smoothed regime registration actually produces;
  `'anderson'` is a solve-free quasi-Newton mixing that converges where Picard
  genuinely stalls ($\|\nabla s\| \to 1$). Use Anderson only once Picard plateaus.
- **`return_residual`** (**HOST-QA** when its scalar is read) — the failure mode is
  a *silent* non-converged inverse returned at the iteration cap. Passing
  `return_residual=True` also returns the realised inversion error
  $\mathrm{rms}\bigl((\mathrm{id}+s)\circ(\mathrm{id}+s_{\text{inv}}) -
  \mathrm{id}\bigr)$ so the caller can **assert** convergence rather than trust it.

### The diffeomorphism square root {#sec-sqrt}

The inversion fixed point has a sibling that powers the logarithm: the
**diffeomorphism square root**, the half-way warp $w$ with $(\mathrm{id}+w) \circ
(\mathrm{id}+w) = \mathrm{id}+s$ (i.e. `compose_displacement(w, w) = s`). It is the
internal `_diffeo_sqrt` helper, and its construction is a small lesson in
fixed-point design. The naive iteration $w \leftarrow s - w\circ(\mathrm{id}+w)$
has a near-identity Jacobian of $-I$ and **oscillates**. The cure is a single idea:
**half-damping**,
$$
w \leftarrow w + \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(s - \texttt{compose\_displacement}(w, w)\bigr),
\qquad w_0 = s/2,
$$ {#eq-sqrt}
which makes the near-identity Jacobian $0$, turning the iteration **super-linear**
(residual $\sim 10^{-10}$ in well under ten steps even for a large deformation) —
plain Picard, no acceleration needed.

### The SVF logarithm, and why `exp` is not onto {#sec-log}

`field_log(displacement, ...)` (**DIFF-JAX**, `geometry.deformation`) is the
inverse of `integrate_velocity_field`: given a deformation $\varphi = \mathrm{id} +
s$, recover the stationary velocity $v$ with $\exp(v) = \varphi$. It is **inverse
scaling-and-squaring** — the dense analogue of `linalg.matrix_log`. Run the
exponential backwards: take $n_{\sqrt{}}$ diffeomorphism square roots (@eq-sqrt)
until the warp is near identity, then $\exp$ of a near-identity field is just the
field itself, so $v \approx 2^{n_{\sqrt{}}} \cdot w$ (the small square-root
displacement scaled back up by the doublings you undid).

Now the caveat the docstring is emphatic about, and that every group-mode recipe
depends on: **`exp` is not surjective.** A general diffeomorphism — in particular a
greedy composition of many *non-commuting* warps, which is exactly what group mode
accumulates — is **not** $\exp(v)$ for any single stationary $v$. The SVFs form a
submanifold of the diffeomorphism group, not all of it. So `field_log` returns the
**best SVF fit**, and you must hold two distinct accuracy notions apart:

- **Round-trip fidelity** — `integrate_velocity_field(field_log(s),
  n_steps=n_sqrt) == id + s` is **exact** (to `sqrt_tol`), *unconditionally*,
  because $v/2^{n_{\sqrt{}}}$ *is* the square-root displacement by construction.
  This is what reproduces the given warp — what most consumers (and the recipes'
  velocity recovery) need.
- **Generating-velocity fidelity** — if $\varphi$ *is* $\exp(v_{\text{true}})$,
  then $v = v_{\text{true}} + O(\|v\|^2/2^{n_{\sqrt{}}})$ (the log approximation,
  halving with each extra root); on a *non-SVF* $\varphi$ there is no
  $v_{\text{true}}$ and the fit carries a nonzero residual $\|\exp(v)-\varphi\|$.

These two pull `correction` in opposite directions. `'first_order'` (default) sets
$v = 2^{n_{\sqrt{}}}\cdot w$ and **preserves the exact round-trip**; `'bch'` applies
the one-term correction $v = 2^{n_{\sqrt{}}}(w - \tfrac{1}{2}(w\cdot\nabla)w)$ — a
better *generating* velocity, but it **breaks** the exact round-trip. Use `'bch'`
only when the velocity is the object of interest (geodesic / momentum analysis, the
velocity barycentre that feeds `geometry.velocity_mean` / `transform_mean`); keep
`'first_order'` whenever you intend to re-exponentiate and recover the warp.

## Keeping the map physical: regularisers {#sec-regularisers}

A dense field with $N$ free vectors will happily over-fit noise into a jagged,
near-folding mess that matches intensities and means nothing anatomically. The cure
is **regularisation** — adding a smoothness / physicality term so the optimiser
prefers a *coherent* deformation. There are two distinct ways this enters, and the
module ships both.

### Explicit penalties {#sec-penalties}

These are scalar energies you **add to the objective** (the
[data term + regulariser](foundations.qmd#sec-problem) of the unit's governing
equation) — for a directly-optimised or learned warp where you control the loss.
All three live in `register.regulariser`, take a channels-last displacement field,
are **DIFF-JAX**, and share a `reduction` argument (`'mean'` default, `'sum'`, or
`'none'` for the per-voxel energy map). The equations below show the per-voxel
energy summed over the grid — the `'sum'` reduction; the **default `'mean'`** is
that sum divided by the voxel count, which keeps the penalty weight resolution- and
level-independent (the natural choice across a coarse-to-fine pyramid).

- **`gradient_smoothness(u)`** — the **diffusion** (first-order) penalty, the
  squared Frobenius norm of the displacement Jacobian summed over its entries,
  $$
  \mathcal{R}_{\text{diff}}(u) = \sum_x \|\nabla u(x)\|_F^2
  = \sum_x \sum_{i,j}\Bigl(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j}\Bigr)^2 .
  $$ {#eq-grad-smooth}
  It penalises *slope* — sharp or incoherent flow — and is zero only for a constant
  (translation) field. Cheap and the default workhorse.
- **`bending_energy(u)`** — the **thin-plate** (second-order) penalty, the squared
  Frobenius norm of the per-voxel Hessian (all second partials
  $\partial^2 u_c/\partial x_a\partial x_b$),
  $$
  \mathcal{R}_{\text{bend}}(u) = \sum_x \|\nabla^2 u(x)\|_F^2 .
  $$ {#eq-bending}
  It penalises *curvature*, not slope — so a uniform **affine** flow is **free**
  (its second derivatives vanish). That affine-invariance is the reason to prefer
  it when the true deformation has a strong large-scale linear component you do not
  want to fight; it is computed by differentiating the displacement Jacobian a
  second time.
- **`jacobian_folding_penalty(u)`** — the direct anti-fold term, the rectified
  negative determinant,
  $$
  \mathcal{R}_{\text{fold}}(u) = \sum_x \max\bigl(-\det J(x),\, 0\bigr),
  $$ {#eq-fold-pen}
  zero wherever the map is orientation-preserving ($\det J > 0$) and growing with
  the depth of any fold ($\det J \le 0$). It targets *exactly* the
  @fig-diffeomorphic-jacdet failure, building on `jacobian_det_displacement`.

### Implicit smoothing in the SVF recipes {#sec-implicit}

The diffeomorphic recipes do **not** add these to a loss. They regularise
*implicitly*, by **Gaussian-smoothing** the field every iteration. The Gaussian is
the Green's function of the fluid/diffusion regularisation operator, so smoothing
the update is mathematically equivalent to penalising its non-smoothness — but it
needs no extra gradient and no penalty weight to tune. The recipes use an
**operator splitting** with two smoothings, named for the classic continuum models:

- **fluid** (`sigma_fluid`) smooths each **update** before it is applied — the
  velocity of the flow is regularised, so the *increment* stays coherent
  (viscous-fluid registration);
- **diffusion** (`sigma_diffusion`) smooths the **accumulated** field after the
  update — the total deformation is regularised (elastic / diffusion
  registration).

So one iteration is: compute the raw force; **fluid**-smooth it; add it to (or
compose it onto) the field; **diffusion**-smooth the result. This is `_regularise`
(algebra) / `_group_regularise` (group) in the shared substrate. The group driver
adds a per-step **Jacobian-backtracking guard** (`_step_clamp_diffeo`): halve the
increment until $\det(I+\nabla\delta) > 0.1$ everywhere — a clamp on the *Jacobian*,
because a mere magnitude clamp can still fold, and because the *total*-field
$\det > 0$ QA is necessary but not sufficient (an intermediate compose can fold
while the total stays positive).

### Anisotropy: physical isotropy from voxel spacing {#sec-anisotropy}

Brain volumes are often acquired anisotropically (e.g. $1\times1\times3$ mm). A
Gaussian sigma or an LNCC window measured in *voxels* then spans different
*physical* extents per axis, biasing the registration. The fix threaded through the
whole SVF substrate is the **relative spacing** $\text{spacing}/\text{geomean}
(\text{spacing})$: level-independent (the coarse-to-fine scale cancels in the
ratio), exactly $1$ for isotropic data (so isotropic reduces to `None`), and used
to make the per-axis sigma and the LNCC window *physically* isotropic
($\sigma_{\text{axis}} = \sigma / r_{\text{axis}}$). The velocity field itself stays
voxel-native; only the *physics* (smoothing extent, window size, gradient scaling)
is anisotropy-corrected. You pass it once as `SVFSpec.spacing`.

## Driving the field: forces and the dense-field solvers {#sec-forces}

We now have everything to *find* the warp. The matrix recipes minimise an
[`Objective`](optimization.qmd#sec-objective) over a parameter vector; the
dense-field recipes **ascend** a similarity over a velocity field, and the object
that turns the current alignment into a per-voxel velocity update is a **`Force`**.
A `Force` is to the SVF drivers what `Objective` is to the matrix driver: it
decouples the *metric* from the *algorithm*, so one driver runs under any force.

### The Force ADT {#sec-force-adt}

The protocol is a two-step **bind/update** hoist (`register._force`):

```python
bound = force.bind(fixed, ndim=ndim, rel_spacing=rel_spacing)  # hoist fixed-state
u    = bound.update(warped)   # raw per-voxel velocity update (voxel units)
cost = bound.cost(warped)     # scalar similarity (lower is better)
```

`Force.bind(fixed, ...)` returns a `BoundForce` with everything *fixed*-dependent
precomputed (e.g. Demons' $\nabla F$), so the single-sided driver binds **once per
level** and reuses it across iterations; the symmetric driver binds **per step**
(its "fixed" is the other image warped to the midpoint, which changes every
iteration). This is the same hoisting discipline as the matrix `Objective`'s
`ic_reference`, paid only where it is recoverable.

The **force direction** is the chain rule. For a minimisation cost
$c(\text{warped})$, gradient descent on the moving deformation moves along
$$
u = -\frac{\partial c}{\partial(\text{warped})}\cdot\nabla(\text{warped}),
$$ {#eq-force-dir}
since $\partial c/\partial(\text{deformation}) = (\partial c/\partial \text{warped})
\cdot \nabla\text{warped}$ by the chain rule (the warped image's spatial gradient
is the sensitivity of intensity to where you sample). Every closed-form force below
is this expression with the metric-specific $\partial c/\partial\text{warped}$ slotted
in; that is the **parity oracle** — `MetricForce(LNCC)` and the closed-form
`LNCCForce` must agree in *direction* to machine precision.

### The forces {#sec-force-zoo}

All are frozen dataclasses, all **DIFF-JAX** through `bind`/`update`:

- **`DemonsForce(alpha=0.4, backend='jax')`** — the closed-form **efficient
  symmetric (ESM) demons** force, the `diffeomorphic_demons_register` default:
  $$
  u = \frac{(F - M\circ\varphi)\,J}{\|J\|^2 + \alpha^2 (F - M\circ\varphi)^2},
  \qquad J = \tfrac{1}{2}(\nabla F + \nabla(M\circ\varphi)).
  $$ {#eq-demons}
  The symmetric gradient $J$ uses *both* images' gradients (faster convergence than
  the one-sided optical-flow force); $\alpha$ damps the step where the intensity
  difference is large. There is **no inner solve** — that closed form is what keeps
  the diffeomorphic recipe GPU-clean. The denominator vanishes only on a matched
  uniform region (both gradient *and* mismatch zero), where the force is zeroed by
  a double-`where` guard that also keeps the *backward* finite (a bare `diff/denom`
  NaNs the gradient even where the forward is masked). `bind` hoists $\nabla F$.
- **`LNCCForce(radius=2, derivative='exact', backend='jax')`** — the analytic
  **local cross-correlation** force, the `greedy_syn_register` default; ascends the
  windowed CC and is robust to smooth intensity inhomogeneity (which plain SSD is
  not). $\partial(\text{sim})/\partial\text{warped}$ is `metrics.lncc_grad` (computed
  from nine box sums over a $2\,\text{radius}+1$ window). `derivative='exact'`
  (default) is the exact gradient of the summed CC; `derivative='center'` is the
  cheaper ANTs/ITK centre-only convention (five box sums, $\sim$1.5–1.75$\times$
  faster) — a *different* force, not `lncc_grad`'s gradient. `backend` can route the
  `'center'` force to a fused sliding-window Pallas/Triton kernel on a supported GPU
  for large 3-D volumes (the size gate defers small/coarse levels to JAX, where the
  integral image wins); see the [performance page](performance.qmd#sec-kernels).
  The window is made physically isotropic on anisotropic grids (per-axis radius
  $\text{radius}/r_{\text{axis}}$).
- **`MIForce(bins=32, magnitude=0.5, normalized=False, sample_stride=1, ...)`** —
  the closed-form **Mattes mutual-information** force, the fast cross-modal path
  (the fMRIPrep metric): `mi_grad · ∇warped`, the analytic $\partial\text{MI}/
  \partial\text{warped}$, instead of a full `jax.grad` of the soft-histogram cost
  every iteration. Because MI is *not* a spatial mean, its raw $1/N$ gradient has an
  arbitrary metric-scale-dependent magnitude, so the force is **RMS-normalised** to
  a controlled per-voxel `magnitude` (the same controlled step the clamped-SyN /
  unclamped-Demons drivers need). `normalized=True` routes the closed-form
  `metrics.nmi_grad` for Studholme's NMI (the ANTs cross-modal default);
  `sample_stride` estimates the histogram from every $k$-th voxel (ITK "Regular"
  sampling — the histogram scatter is the bottleneck) while applying the force
  densely. Its `range_moving`/`range_fixed` are pinned **once** before the pyramid
  (see `pin_force_ranges` below).
- **`MetricForce(metric, magnitude=0.5)`** — the **escape hatch**: drive a recipe
  by $-\partial(\text{metric.cost})/\partial\text{warped}\cdot\nabla\text{warped}$
  via `jax.grad` of *any* [`Metric`](similarity.qmd#sec-ssd), so a metric with no
  hand-written force still registers diffeomorphically. *No performance guarantee* —
  the autodiff tape is the price of generality. Its magnitude logic keys on
  `metric.is_spatial_mean`: for a **spatial-mean** cost (SSD/LNCC) it rescales the
  gradient by the voxel count to undo the $1/N$, recovering the *sum-convention*
  gradient — so `MetricForce(LNCC(r))` is numerically **identical** to
  `LNCCForce(r)` (the magnitude parity oracle); for a **global histogram scalar**
  (MI/CR) it RMS-normalises to `magnitude` instead. Note `MetricForce(SSD)` is the
  *raw* optical-flow gradient, **not** the normalised Demons force — for monomodal
  SSD-type work prefer `DemonsForce`.
- **`SumForce(terms)`** — the **combinator**: a weighted sum of forces (the
  dense-field analogue of ANTs `-m MI[...] -m CC[...]` in one stage). `update` is
  $\sum_i w_i\cdot\text{force}_i.\text{update}$ and `cost` the matching weighted
  sum; it is itself a `Force`, so it composes any of the above (mixing tiers freely,
  e.g. a structural `LNCCForce` plus a cross-modal `MIForce`) with no driver change.
  Each term is bound once.

`resolve_force_schedule(force, default=..., levels=...)` turns the recipe's `force`
argument into a per-level list: `None` → the recipe's closed-form `default` at every
level; a single `Force` → that force everywhere; a length-`levels`
**coarse-to-fine** sequence → one force per level (reversed to the finest-first
pyramid indexing the driver uses) — e.g. a cheap force at the coarse levels, a
high-signal one at the finest. `pin_force_ranges(force, moving, fixed)` is the
companion **HOST-CTOR**-ish hoist: it resolves any `None` intensity range on an
`MIForce` **once**, before the pyramid, from the full-resolution images (a
`stop_gradient`-ed reduction, so it is jit-safe and the bin edges stay constant) —
because a data min/max range *drifts* as the moving image deforms, a non-stationary
objective.

### The coarse-to-fine drivers {#sec-drivers}

The shared spine is `svf_coarse_to_fine` (`register._svf`): it carries a tuple of
$n_{\text{fields}}$ voxel-unit velocity (or displacement) fields from the coarsest
[Gaussian-pyramid](resampling.qmd#sec-pyramid) level to the finest, **prolonging**
between levels (an interpolating upsample times the per-axis voxel-scale ratio that
keeps the field in voxel units), and delegating the per-level update to a
`level_solve` callback. The four level kernels are the algebra/group $\times$
single-sided/symmetric matrix:

- **`single_sided_level`** (Demons structure, algebra) — warp `moving` by
  $\exp(v)$, bind the force to `fixed` **once**, and drive $v$ up the similarity:
  `exp` → warp → fluid-smooth the force → log-update ($v + u$ or BCH) →
  diffusion-smooth $v$. Rolled with `lax.scan`.
- **`symmetric_level`** (SyN structure, algebra) — warp **both** images to the
  shared midpoint, bind the force **per step** (each image's "fixed" is the other at
  the midpoint), and ascend the similarity in each direction, regularising both
  half-velocities. Returns `(v_fwd, v_inv, costs)`.
- **`group_single_sided_level`** / **`group_symmetric_level`** — the group-domain
  siblings: carry the displacement(s), warp **directly** by $\mathrm{id}+s$ (no
  `exp`), and **compose** the regularised increment onto $s$ ($\sim$2 / $\sim$4
  gathers per iteration vs $\sim$7 / $\sim$12 for algebra). They drop `n_steps` /
  `bch_order` (no integration, no log-domain BCH), and the inverse / velocity are
  recovered only **once** at finalisation — a different driver, not a re-parametrised
  one.

The masking and axis-restriction knobs ride every level fn: `mask` gates the force
to a region (the masked area drives, the rest follows by regularisation);
`restrict` (length-`ndim`) weights the force per spatial axis so a `0` *persistently*
suppresses deformation along that axis (e.g. `(1, 1, 0)` for in-plane-only — the
zeroed component never enters the additive update, and the spatial-only smoothing
cannot reintroduce it). The trust-region step control (`_normalise_step`) is the
SyN convention: a robust (99th-percentile, outlier-proof) cap on the per-voxel
displacement so the warp stays diffeomorphic under a fixed iteration budget —
`step_mode='clamp'` ($\min(1, \text{step}/\|u\|_{\text{cap}})$, never amplifies) or
`'normalize'` (the ANTs magnitude-invariant scale-to-step, which avoids
under-stepping a small-magnitude force like the centre-only LNCC).

### The two recipes {#sec-recipes}

**`diffeomorphic_demons_register(moving, fixed, *, spec=DemonsSpec(), ...)`**
(`register.diffeomorphic`) is the log-domain Demons recipe (Vercauteren 2009): a
single stationary velocity, the closed-form ESM `DemonsForce` by default, SSD-class
matching, coarse-to-fine. Returns a `DiffeomorphicResult(velocity, displacement,
warped, jacobian_det, cost_history)`. `DemonsSpec` adds `n_steps` (scaling-and-
squaring count), `alpha` (force normalisation), and `bch_order` (1 additive / 2
with $\tfrac12[v,u]$) to the shared `SVFSpec`.

**`greedy_syn_register(moving, fixed, *, spec=SyNSpec(), ...)`** (`register._syn`)
is the greedy symmetric SyN recipe (Avants 2008, the greedy gradient-flow variant —
no geodesic shooting), the diffeomorphic-quality jump: two velocities $v_{\text{fwd}}$
(moving → midpoint) and $v_{\text{inv}}$ (fixed → midpoint), the LNCC force by
default, robust to intensity inhomogeneity, with no moving/fixed asymmetry. The
final moving → fixed map is the **midpoint composition**
$$
\varphi = (\mathrm{id} + s_{\text{fwd}}) \circ (\mathrm{id} + s_{\text{inv}})^{-1},
$$ {#eq-syn}
built from the two halves via `invert_displacement` + `compose_displacement` — a
diffeomorphism **by construction** (each half is, and composition of
diffeomorphisms is one). The single inversion stays at finalisation in group mode
(not per step). `SyNSpec` adds `radius`, `step`, `step_mode`, `n_steps`.

Both recipes share the same front matter: `preprocess_images` (optional
`winsorize` / `histogram_match` intensity conditioning), `resolve_init_displacement`
+ `prewarp_moving` for an `init_affine` or `init_displacement` **warm-start** (the
[matrix recipes'](recipes.qmd#sec-matrix-recipes) output, or a SynthMorph field —
`moving` is pre-warped onto the `fixed` grid and only the *residual* is registered),
optional per-level `smoothing_sigma` (ANTs `-s`, decoupled from the shrink), and
`finalize_with_init` (compose the residual with the init, warp the *original*
moving, return the **total**-map Jacobian determinant). Both are pure
$(\text{moving}, \text{fixed}) \to \text{result}$ functions, so a **cohort**
registers to a shared template with a single `jax.vmap` over the moving stack — the
`mode='early_exit'` `while_loop` then runs (via `vmap`) to the all-lanes exit, the
slowest subject setting the trip count.

One reproducibility caveat both flag: the vector-field Gaussian regulariser
dispatches engines per backend (parallel FIR on GPU, recursive Young–van Vliet on
CPU), which differ by $\sim$1–2% near the edges — so the *same* pair can yield
slightly different deformations on GPU vs CPU (recovery accuracy is unaffected).
Register every subject of a study on the **same backend**; do not mix CPU- and
GPU-computed warps in one analysis.

### Back to the motivating question {#sec-payoff}

For the inter-subject cortical task: affine-align first, hand the affine to
`greedy_syn_register` as `init_affine`, and let the LNCC-driven symmetric flow match
the sulci. The result's `displacement` warps subject A onto B, `warped` is A
resampled into correspondence, and — the test that matters — `jacobian_det`
**positive everywhere** certifies the cortical map did not tear or fold. That
guarantee is not luck; it is the SVF construction (@eq-exp), the
diffeomorphism-by-construction midpoint composition (@eq-syn), and the
implicit smoothing, working together.

## Further reading

- Arsigny, V., Commowick, O., Pennec, X., & Ayache, N. (2006). *A log-Euclidean
  framework for statistics on diffeomorphisms.* MICCAI. The stationary-velocity /
  log-Euclidean viewpoint — `exp`, `log`, and the BCH composition behind
  `compose_velocity` and `field_log`.
- Vercauteren, T., Pennec, X., Perchant, A., & Ayache, N. (2009). *Diffeomorphic
  demons: efficient non-parametric image registration.* NeuroImage, 45(1, Supp. 1).
  The log-domain Demons recipe and the efficient-symmetric (ESM) force of
  @eq-demons.
- Avants, B. B., Epstein, C. L., Grossman, M., & Gee, J. C. (2008). *Symmetric
  diffeomorphic image registration with cross-correlation.* Medical Image Analysis,
  12(1). SyN — the symmetric midpoint construction (@eq-syn) and the local-CC
  driving force the greedy recipe approaches.
- Beg, M. F., Miller, M. I., Trouvé, A., & Younes, L. (2005). *Computing large
  deformation metric mappings via geodesics on flows of diffeomorphisms.*
  International Journal of Computer Vision, 61(2). LDDMM — the geodesic-shooting
  formulation the *greedy* SyN variant simplifies (gradient flow + Gaussian
  regularisation, no shooting).
- Ashburner, J. (2007). *A fast diffeomorphic image registration algorithm
  (DARTEL).* NeuroImage, 38(1). Scaling-and-squaring of a stationary velocity field
  at scale — the algorithm behind `integrate_velocity_field`.
- Higham, N. J. (2008). *Functions of Matrices: Theory and Computation.* SIAM. The
  scaling-and-squaring exp/log analysis (@eq-halving, @eq-ss) in the matrix setting
  `field_log` mirrors densely.
- Thirion, J.-P. (1998). *Image matching as a diffusion process: an analogy with
  Maxwell's demons.* Medical Image Analysis, 2(3). The original demons force and the
  fluid/diffusion regularisation split the recipes use.
- Modersitzki, J. (2004). *Numerical Methods for Image Registration.* Oxford
  University Press. The elastic / fluid / diffusion regularisers (@eq-grad-smooth,
  @eq-bending) as variational energies.

## See also

- Source — the velocity/displacement algebra (`compose_displacement`,
  `compose_velocity`, `invert_displacement`, `field_log`, the diffeomorphism square
  root):
  [`src/nitrix/geometry/deformation.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/deformation.py).
- Source — `integrate_velocity_field` (scaling-and-squaring), `identity_grid`,
  `spatial_transform`, `jacobian_displacement`, `jacobian_det_displacement`:
  [`src/nitrix/geometry/grid.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/grid.py).
- Source — the explicit penalties (`gradient_smoothness`, `bending_energy`,
  `jacobian_folding_penalty`):
  [`src/nitrix/register/regulariser.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/regulariser.py).
- Source — the Force ADT and the closed-form / generic forces (`DemonsForce`,
  `LNCCForce`, `MIForce`, `MetricForce`, `SumForce`, `resolve_force_schedule`):
  [`src/nitrix/register/_force.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_force.py).
- Source — the SVF substrate (`svf_coarse_to_fine`, the single-sided / symmetric /
  group level kernels, `SVFSpec`, the regularisation and step-clamp helpers):
  [`src/nitrix/register/_svf.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_svf.py).
- Source — the log-Demons recipe (`diffeomorphic_demons_register`, `DemonsSpec`,
  `DiffeomorphicResult`):
  [`src/nitrix/register/diffeomorphic.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/diffeomorphic.py).
- Source — the greedy-SyN recipe (`greedy_syn_register`, `SyNSpec`, `SyNResult`):
  [`src/nitrix/register/_syn.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_syn.py).
- Source — the differentiable fixed-point solver behind `invert_displacement` /
  `field_log`:
  [`src/nitrix/numerics/fixed_point.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/numerics/fixed_point.py).
- Previous in reading order: [the optimisation engine](optimization.qmd#sec-objective)
  (the matrix-recipe sibling of this page's drivers) and
  [similarity metrics](similarity.qmd#sec-lncc) (the LNCC / MI the forces ascend).
  Next: [surface registration](surface-registration.qmd#sec-parameterize). The
  recipe-level configuration and SVF cookbook live in
  [recipes](recipes.qmd#sec-svf-recipes); the force kernels and backends in
  [performance](performance.qmd#sec-kernels). Sibling units:
  [geometry](../geometry/foundations.qmd), [statistics](../statistics/foundations.qmd).
